6: YHWH YHWH el rachum vechanun erech apayim verav chesed veemet: 'YHWH YHWH, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love [chesed] and faithfulness [emet].' The thirteen attributes of divine mercy (mid-dot harachamim) became a cornerstone of Jewish liturgical theology, recited on fast days and festivals. Chesed (covenant love, steadfast love, lovingkindness) is perhaps the OT's richest theological term — it combines loyalty, love, mercy, and covenant-faithfulness into a single word. John's 'grace and truth' (John 1:14, 17) is likely a translation of chesed veemet, applying Exodus 34:6's divine attributes to the incarnate Word.
1: pesol lĕkā šĕnê lûḥōt ʾăbānîm kāriʾšōnîm: 'Cut for yourself two tablets of stone like the first ones.' The new tablets require Moses's own labor (pesol lĕkā = cut for yourself) — unlike the first tablets, which were entirely YHWH's work (Exodus 32:16: 'the work of God'). The writing is still YHWH's (wĕkātabtî ʾal-halûḥōt = I will write on the tablets), but the preparation of the material is now Moses's task. This shift in the covenant renewal: the second covenant involves human participation in preparation even while the content remains divine.
2: Moses is commanded to be ready in the morning (wĕhāyîtā nākôn labbōqer) and come up to Mount Sinai. The morning ascent pattern — the same time when the manna was gathered and the Sabbath morning designated for rest — carries the rhythm of covenant renewal into the liturgical cycle. Moses ascends alone (wĕʾîš lōʾ yiʿăleh ʿimmāk); the covenant renewal is a private encounter before its public declaration.
3: wĕʾîš lōʾ-yirʾeh bĕkol-hāhār gam-haṣṣōʾn wĕhabbāqār ʾal-yirʿû ʾel-mûl hāhār hahûʾ: 'no one shall appear in all the mountain; neither flocks nor herds shall graze before that mountain.' The comprehensive exclusion — no human, no animal — recreates the holiness boundary of the original Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:12-13). The renewal demands the same awe as the original institution. The covenant is not a casual recondition; it requires the full formal apparatus of holy encounter.
4: Moses cuts the two tablets, rises early in the morning, and ascends Sinai with the two tablets in hand (bĕyādô). The combination of obedience (cuts as commanded, rises early) and preparation (brings the tablets) enacts Moses as the faithful mediator. His actions model the covenant response: doing exactly as YHWH commanded, without delay or modification.
5: wayyērēd YHWH beʿānān wayyityaṣṣēb ʿimmô šām wayyiqrāʾ bĕšēm YHWH: 'YHWH descended in the cloud and stood with him there, and proclaimed the name of YHWH.' The cloud descent is the theophanic vehicle; the proclamation of the divine name is the content. The phrase wayyiqrāʾ bĕšēm YHWH is ambiguous — it could be YHWH proclaiming his own name, or Moses calling on YHWH's name. The context favors YHWH's self-proclamation: what follows in Exodus 34:6 is the definitive self-disclosure of the divine character.
7: nōṣēr ḥesed lāʾlāpîm nōśēʾ ʿāwōn wāpeša wĕḥaṭṭāʾāh wĕnaqqēh lōʾ yĕnaqqeh pōqēd ʿăwōn ʾābōt ʿal-bānîm: 'keeping steadfast love for thousands, bearing iniquity and transgression and sin, and by no means clearing the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation.' The asymmetry is deliberate and theologically crucial: mercy extends to thousands (lāʾlāpîm = to the thousandth generation), but punishment only to the third and fourth generation. The three sin-terms — ʿāwōn (iniquity, moral distortion), peša (transgression, rebellion), ḥaṭṭāʾāh (sin, missing the mark) — together cover the full spectrum of human wrongdoing. YHWH does not simply set aside guilt (wĕnaqqēh lōʾ yĕnaqqeh = 'by no means clearing/acquitting') — this tension between mercy and justice reaches its resolution only in the atonement.
8: Moses immediately bows and worships (wayyiqqōd mōšeh ʾarṣāh wayyištaḥû). The response to the proclamation of the divine character is prostration — the knowledge of YHWH's mercy and holiness together produces not familiarity but awe. The thirteen attributes (Exodus 34:6-7) are not abstract propositions but the living character of the God before whom Moses falls.
9: Moses intercedes immediately: if I have found favor, let YHWH go in Israel's midst, even though they are stiff-necked (ʿam qĕšēh ʾōrep). The appeal is to the very mercy just proclaimed: YHWH has declared himself forgiving of iniquity and transgression — Moses calls YHWH to act in accordance with his own character. Wĕsālaḥtā lăʿăwōnēnû ûlĕḥaṭṭāʾtēnû ûnĕḥaltānû: 'forgive our iniquity and sin and take us for your inheritance.' The three petitions correspond to the three sin-terms of Exodus 34:7 — Moses is asking YHWH to exercise toward Israel exactly the mercy he has just claimed as his character.
10: hinnēh ʾānōkî kōrēt bĕrît: 'Behold, I am making a covenant.' YHWH responds to Moses's intercession with covenant renewal. The covenant is not a renegotiated form but the same covenant on new tablets — the content of YHWH's commitment is unchanged; the relationship is restored. Niplāʾōt ʾăšer lōʾ-nibĕrĕʾû bĕkol-hāʾāreṣ: 'wonders that have not been created in all the earth' — the renewed covenant is accompanied by miraculous signs that distinguish Israel from all other nations. The covenant is the source of Israel's unique history.
11: The covenant stipulations begin with expulsion of the Canaanite nations. Šĕmor lĕkā ʾēt ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawwĕkā hayyôm: 'Observe what I command you today.' The renewed covenant has the same stipulation structure as the original: grace, then obligation. YHWH will drive out the nations; Israel must not make covenants with them or intermarry. The covenant relationship demands exclusive allegiance.
12: hišāmer lĕkā pen-tikkĕrōt bĕrît lĕyôšēb hāʾāreṣ: 'Take care, lest you make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land.' The danger of covenant entanglement with Canaan is the specific warning: their altars must be torn down, their pillars broken, their Asherah poles cut down. The exclusivity of the covenant with YHWH requires the destruction of all alternatives — not toleration, not negotiation, but dismantling.
13: mīzbĕḥōtēhem tiṯṯōṣûn ûmaṣṣĕbōtām tĕšabbērûn wĕʾăšērāw tikrōtûn: three verbs of destruction targeting three types of pagan installation: altars (mīzbĕḥōt), sacred pillars (maṣṣĕbôt), and Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm). Each type targets a different aspect of Canaanite worship: the altar (sacrifice site), the standing stone (deity's presence marker), the wooden pole (fertility goddess symbol). The comprehensive dismantling leaves no residue of alternative worship systems.
14: kî YHWH qannāʾ šĕmô ʾēl qannāʾ hûʾ: 'For YHWH, whose name is Jealous (qannāʾ), is a jealous God.' The divine name 'Jealous' appears only here and in Exodus 20:5. Qannāʾ (from qānāʾ, to be jealous/zealous) describes YHWH's passionate, exclusive claim on Israel's allegiance — the covenantal love that cannot tolerate a rival. The use of qannāʾ as a divine name (not merely an attribute) indicates this is constitutive of who YHWH is, not a mood he occasionally enters. NT application: Christ claims the same exclusivity ('no one comes to the Father except through me,' John 14:6).
15: The specific danger of covenant with Canaanite peoples: they will invite Israel to eat their sacrifices, and Israel's sons will take their daughters as wives. The warning traces the path of apostasy: shared meals at pagan altars → intermarriage → idolatry through the spouse's influence. The same concern appears in Solomon's apostasy (1 Kings 11:1-13) and the Ezra-Nehemiah crisis (Ezra 9-10) — the historical record validates the prophylactic warning.
16: wĕlāqaḥtā mibĕnōtāw lĕbānêkā wĕzānû bĕnōtāw ʾaḥărê ʾĕlōhêhen wĕhiznû ʾet-bānêkā ʾaḥărê ʾĕlōhêhen: 'and when you take their daughters for your sons, and their daughters prostitute themselves after their gods, they will make your sons prostitute themselves after their gods.' The verb zānāh (to prostitute/play the harlot) applied to apostasy is the dominant metaphor of Hosea, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel — Israel's covenant relationship with YHWH is a marriage, so other-god worship is adultery.
17: ʾĕlōhê massēkāh lōʾ taʿăśeh lāk: 'You shall not make cast idols for yourself.' The prohibition directly references the golden calf incident (massēkāh = cast/poured metal image, the same word used in Exodus 32:4). The renewed covenant is a covenant for a people who have already violated this command and been forgiven — the repetition is a reminder in the context of grace, not merely legal reiteration.
18: The Festival of Unleavened Bread (ḥag hammaṣṣôt) and its seven-day observance in the month of Abib. The calendar of Israel's worship is embedded in the covenant renewal: the festivals that commemorate YHWH's saving acts are part of the covenant stipulations. ʾĕt ḥōdeš hāʾābîb (in the month of Abib — the spring month of the Exodus) — the Passover month anchors the entire liturgical year in the formative redemptive event.
19: The firstborn law: all that opens the womb belongs to YHWH. The principle of consecration of first fruits — the first of everything belongs to the One who gives everything — applies to livestock as well as people. Bekôr (firstborn) is the theological category established in the Exodus itself: YHWH struck Egypt's firstborn to redeem Israel his firstborn son (Exodus 4:22-23; Exodus 11:5; Exodus 13:2).
20: The firstborn donkey redeemed with a lamb; if not redeemed, its neck is broken. The human firstborn must be redeemed. Wĕlōʾ-yērāʾû pānay rêqām: 'and they shall not appear before me empty-handed.' The covenant community comes before YHWH bringing something — the firstfruits that acknowledge his ownership. Appearing empty-handed (without offering) is the refusal to acknowledge covenant relationship. The offering is not earning favor but expressing dependence.
21: šēšet yāmîm taʿăbōd ûbayyôm haššĕbîʿî tišbōt bĕḥāriš ûbaqāṣîr tišbōt: 'Six days you shall work, but on the seventh day you shall rest; in plowing time and in harvest you shall rest.' The Sabbath law includes the most demanding seasons of agricultural labor — plowing and harvest. The Sabbath is not a convenience but a challenge: rest even when work is most urgent, trusting YHWH's provision over human effort. The sabbatical principle reshapes the agricultural economy around covenant time rather than production demand.
22: Three harvest festivals: ḥag šābuʿōt (Festival of Weeks, seven weeks after Passover = Pentecost), ḥag hāʾāsîp (Festival of Ingathering = Tabernacles), and the transition of the year (tĕqûpat haššānāh). The three pilgrimage festivals structure Israel's entire agricultural year as a series of appearances before YHWH. The calendar of Israel is not merely a timekeeping system but a theology of providence — each harvest is a gift to be acknowledged before the Giver.
23: Three times a year all males shall appear before YHWH, the God of Israel. The pilgrimage obligation — leaving home, going to the sanctuary — is the embodied enactment of covenant priority. YHWH is called hāʾādôn YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl (the Lord YHWH God of Israel) — the full covenant identification. The triannual appearance is the covenant community's repeated acknowledgment of who their Lord is.
24: The promise accompanying the pilgrimage obligation: YHWH will drive out the nations before Israel and enlarge its territory. The vulnerability of pilgrimage — the land unattended while everyone goes to the sanctuary — is met with a divine security guarantee: wĕlōʾ yaḥmod ʾîš ʾet-ʾarṣĕkā bĕʿālōtĕkā ('no one shall covet your land when you go up'). YHWH's provision during human absence is the structural expression of covenant trust: appearing before YHWH creates the condition for YHWH to protect what is entrusted to him.
25: lōʾ-tišḥaṭ ʿal-ḥāmēṣ dam zibḥî: 'You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leaven.' The purity requirements for sacrifice: no leaven mixed with the blood offering (cf. Exodus 12:15-20 — leaven is excluded from Passover). The separation of the sacred from ordinary bread (leaven is part of daily life) maintains the distinctiveness of covenant worship. The same principle of separation underlies the NT's 'purge out the old leaven' (1 Corinthians 5:7-8).
26: The first of the firstfruits of the ground shall be brought to the house of YHWH. Lōʾ tĕbaššēl gĕdî baḥălēb ʾimmô: 'You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk.' The prohibition — which appears three times (Exodus 13:19; Exodus 23:19; here) — protects against the use of the mother's milk (which is for sustaining life) as the medium of the offspring's death. It may also distinguish Israelite practice from Canaanite sacrificial customs. The triple repetition in the Torah signals its importance for covenant identity.
27: kĕtāb lĕkā ʾet-hadĕbārîm hāʾēlleh kî ʿal-pî hadĕbārîm hāʾēlleh kāraʾtî ʾittĕkā bĕrît wĕʾet-yiśrāʾēl: 'Write these words, for in accordance with these words I have made a covenant with you and with Israel.' YHWH commands Moses to write — the covenant is a written document, not merely oral tradition. The written word is the enduring form of the covenant. The parallel to the Sinai tablets: YHWH wrote on stone, Moses writes these words; the covenant has both divine inscription and human transcription.
28: wayhî šām ʿim YHWH ʾarbāʿîm yôm wĕʾarbāʿîm laylāh leḥem lōʾ ʾākal ûmayim lōʾ šātāh: 'He was there with YHWH forty days and forty nights; he neither ate bread nor drank water.' The second forty-day fast mirrors the first (Exodus 24:18) — both covenant encounters require the same duration of concentrated divine presence. The abstention from food and water is not ascetic virtue but the physical sign of being entirely sustained by the divine presence. Jesus's forty-day fast in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2) recapitulates Moses's Sinai fasts — the new covenant mediator undergoes the same probationary discipline as the old.
29: wĕlōʾ yādaʿ mōšeh kî qāran ʿôr pĕnê mōšeh bĕdabbĕrô ʾittô: 'Moses did not know that the skin of his face shone (qāran) because he had been speaking with him.' The verb qāran (to emit rays, to shine) derives from qeren (horn/ray) — Moses's face emitted rays of light after the divine encounter. The unconscious nature of the transformation (lōʾ yādaʿ) is significant: the glory is not Moses's achievement but an effect of contact with YHWH's presence. Paul's extended meditation on this verse in 2 Corinthians 3:7-18 contrasts the fading glory (the veil hides what is passing away) with the permanent, unveiled glory of the new covenant.
30: Aaron and all the children of Israel saw Moses: wĕhinnēh qāran ʿôr pānāyw wayyîrĕʾû miggešet ʾēlāyw — 'his face was shining, and they were afraid to come near him.' The radiance creates a distance — the reflected glory makes Moses approachable only with preparation. The fear response is the human reaction to mediated divine holiness: even the derivative glory (Moses reflecting YHWH's glory) is beyond ordinary human comfort.
31: Moses calls Aaron and the leaders, and they return to him. He gives them the commandments just received. The structured order of proclamation — leaders first, then the whole community — establishes the pattern of covenant transmission: from the mediator to the elders to the people. The same pattern governs the delivery of the law throughout Deuteronomy.
32: After that, all the children of Israel approach and Moses commands them all that YHWH had spoken with him on Sinai. The completeness of transmission is emphasized: Moses passes on everything, not a selection. Kol ʾăšer dibbēr YHWH ʾittô bĕhar Sînāy — 'all that YHWH had spoken with him on Mount Sinai.' The chain of revelation: YHWH → Moses → leaders → people. Each link transmits without loss.
33: ûkĕkallōt mōšeh lĕdabbēr ʾittām nātan ʿal-pānāyw masweh: 'when Moses had finished speaking with them, he put a veil (masweh) on his face.' The word masweh (veil/covering) is a hapax legomenon in the OT — this is its only occurrence. The veil is put on after the speaking, not before — during proclamation Moses is unveiled, but between sessions of covenant instruction his face is covered. Paul's reading of the veil in 2 Corinthians 3:13-18 interprets it as hiding the fading glory; but textually, it also protects the people from the unmediated radiance that frightened them (Exodus 34:30).
34: ûbĕbōʾ mōšeh lipnê YHWH lĕdabbēr ʾittô yāsîr ʾet-hammasweh ʿad ṣēʾtô: 'when Moses went in before YHWH to speak with him, he took off the veil until he came out.' The veil protocol: Moses removes it when entering YHWH's presence (face-to-face access to the divine), keeps it on when among the people between instruction sessions. The veil marks the boundary between the two registers of Moses's existence — priestly access to YHWH and mediatorial communication to Israel.
35: wĕrāʾû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-pĕnê mōšeh kî qāran ʿôr pĕnê mōšeh: 'the children of Israel saw the face of Moses, that the skin of Moses's face shone.' The pattern repeats each time Moses emerges from the divine presence — the shining is renewed with each encounter. The glory is not permanent on Moses; it requires repeated visits. Paul's contrast: the new covenant's glory is doxa ek doxes, glory from glory, permanently transforming (2 Corinthians 3:18: 'beholding the glory of the Lord, we are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another').
